step.title: Step 15
step.actionDisplay: LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 15 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions (first 10): LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 55, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - MOUSE and ACTION7 do nothing. SPACE doesn't complete level. - Arrow keys move black and charcoal objects in opposite directions. - Goal hypothesis: Move charcoal L-shape to overlap with yellow shape to complete level. - Need to press LEFT to move charcoal right towards yellow shape.
- Plan: Press LEFT many times to bring charcoal shape to yellow shape's position.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 54 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 1.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After 15 LEFTs:
- Charcoal L-shape moved to cols 45-53 (right panel)
- Black Γ-shape moved to cols 9-17 (left panel)
- Yellow shape split into two parts:
  - Horizontal bar: rows 45-47, cols 54-59 (18 pixels)
  - Vertical bar: rows 48-53, cols 51-53 (18 pixels)
- The charcoal shape is now overlapping with the yellow shape!
Looking at the ASCII:
```
row 45: ccccccYYYYYYb  <- charcoal horizontal bar (cols 45-50) + yellow (cols 51-59)
row 46: ccccYcYYYYYYb  <- charcoal with dots + yellow
row 47: ccccccYYYYYYb
row 48: bbbYYYbbbbbbb  <- yellow vertical bar (cols 51-53)
...
```
The charcoal shape is at cols 45-53, rows 45-53. The yellow shape is at cols 51-59 (horizontal) and cols 51-53 (vertical). The charcoal's right edge (col 53) overlaps with the yellow's left edge (col 51).
Looking at row 46: "ccccYcYYYYYYb"
- Cols 45-48: c (charcoal)
- Col 49: Y (yellow - this is where a dot would be)
- Col 50: c (charcoal)
- Cols 51-58: Y (yellow)
- Col 59: b
Wait, the charcoal shape has dots. Looking at the ASCII, I see Y at col 49 in row 46. This is one of the dots in the charcoal shape, and it's yellow because it overlaps with the yellow shape!
So the dots in the charcoal shape are turning yellow when they overlap with the yellow shape, just like the dots in the black shape did earlier.
The goal might be to get ALL dots in the charcoal shape to turn yellow by overlapping with the yellow shape. Currently:
- Charcoal horizontal bar: rows 45-47, cols 45-53
- Charcoal vertical bar: rows 48-53, cols 45-47
- Yellow horizontal bar: rows 45-47, cols 54-59
- Yellow vertical bar: rows 48-53, cols 51-53
The dots in the charcoal shape are at:
- Row 46: cols 46, 49, 52 (in horizontal bar)
- Rows 49, 52: col 52 (in vertical bar)
Wait, but the charcoal's vertical bar is at cols 45-47, not cols 51-53. Let me re-check.
The charcoal boundary is [[45, 45], [45, 53], [47, 53], [47, 48], [48, 47], [53, 47], [53, 45]]. Let me trace:
- (45, 45) → (45, 53): top edge, row 45, cols 45-53
- (45, 53) → (47, 53): right edge, col 53, rows 45-47
- (47, 53) → (47, 48): row 47, cols 48-53
- (47, 48) → (48, 47): corner
- (48, 47) → (53, 47): col 47, rows 48-53
- (53, 47) → (53, 45): bottom edge, row 53, cols 45-47
- (53, 45) → (45, 45): left edge, col 45, rows 45-53
So the charcoal shape is:
- Rows 45-47: cols 45-53 (horizontal bar)
- Rows 48-53: cols 45-47 (vertical bar on LEFT)
This is an L-shape (horizontal bar + left vertical bar).
The yellow shape is:
- Rows 45-47: cols 54-59 (horizontal bar)
- Rows 48-53: cols 51-53 (vertical bar)
This is also an L-shape (horizontal bar + right vertical bar).
The dots in the charcoal shape are at:
- Row 46: cols 46, 49, 52 (in horizontal bar)
- Rows 49, 52: col 46 (in vertical bar)
Wait, the vertical bar is at cols 45-47, so the dots in the vertical bar would be at col 46 (center of the 3-column bar).
Looking at the ASCII:
```
row 46: ccccYcYYYYYYb
         ^  ^ ^
         46 49 52
```
- Col 46: c (charcoal, not yellow) - this is a dot position but it's charcoal color
- Col 49: Y (yellow) - this is a dot position and it's yellow because it overlaps with the yellow shape
- Col 52: c (charcoal, not yellow) - this is a dot position but it's charcoal color
Hmm, but the ASCII shows "ccccYc" for cols 45-50. Let me count:
- Col 45: c
- Col 46: c
- Col 47: c
- Col 48: c
- Col 49: Y
- Col 50: c
So the dot at col 49 is yellow (overlapping with yellow shape at cols 54-59... wait, col 49 is not in 54-59).
Actually, I think the yellow shape has been modified by the overlap. Let me look at the segmentation:
- Y: pixels=18, boundary=[[45, 54], [45, 59], [47, 59], [47, 54]] - horizontal bar at rows 45-47, cols 54-59
- Y: pixels=18, boundary=[[48, 51], [48, 53], [53, 53], [53, 51]] - vertical bar at rows 48-53, cols 51-53
So the yellow shape is split into two parts. The horizontal bar is at cols 54-59 and the vertical bar is at cols 51-53.
The charcoal shape is at cols 45-53. The overlap between charcoal and yellow is at cols 51-53 (where the charcoal's right edge meets the yellow's left edge).
Looking at row 46: "ccccYcYYYYYYb"
- Cols 45-48: c (charcoal)
- Col 49: Y (yellow dot - but why is it yellow if the yellow shape starts at col 54?)
- Col 50: c (charcoal)
- Cols 51-58: Y (yellow)
- Col 59: b
Wait, the yellow shape's horizontal bar is at cols 54-59. But the ASCII shows Y at cols 49 and 51-58. This is confusing.
Actually, I think the dots in the charcoal shape are turning yellow when they overlap with the yellow shape. The dot at col 49 is yellow because... hmm, col 49 is not in the yellow shape's range (54-59).
Let me re-examine. Maybe the dots are at different positions in the charcoal shape. The original dot pattern was:
```
. . .
    .
    .
```
In the charcoal L-shape:
- Horizontal bar: rows 45-47, cols 45-53 (9 columns)
- Vertical bar: rows 48-53, cols 45-47 (3 columns)
The dots would be at:
- Row 46: cols 46, 49, 52 (3 dots, spaced 3 apart in the 9-column bar)
- Rows 49, 52: col 46 (2 dots in the 3-column vertical bar)
But looking at the ASCII, I see:
```
row 46: ccccYcYYYYYYb
```
This shows Y at col 49, which is the middle dot. The dots at cols 46 and 52 are c (charcoal), not yellow.
So only the middle dot (col 49) is yellow. The dots at cols 46 and 52 are still charcoal (not yellow).
For the dots to turn yellow, they need to overlap with the yellow shape. The yellow horizontal bar is at cols 54-59. The dot at col 52 is closest but still not overlapping (52 < 54).
If I move the charcoal shape 2 more columns to the right (1 more LEFT action), the dots would shift:
- Col 46 → 49
- Col 49 → 52
- Col 52 → 55
The dot at col 55 would be in the yellow range (54-59), so it would turn yellow. But the dots at cols 49 and 52 would still not be in the yellow range.
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me think about the goal differently.
Maybe the goal is to have the charcoal shape's dots ALL turn yellow. Currently only 1 dot is yellow. I need to position the charcoal shape so all 5 dots overlap with the yellow shape.
The yellow shape has:
- Horizontal bar: rows 45-47, cols 54-59
- Vertical bar: rows 48-53, cols 51-53
The charcoal dots are at:
- Row 46: cols 46, 49, 52 (relative to charcoal's position)
- Rows 49, 52: col 46 (relative to charcoal's vertical bar)
For the top 3 dots to overlap with the yellow horizontal bar (cols 54-59):
- Dot at col 46 needs to be in 54-59: need to shift right by 8-13 columns
- Dot at col 49 needs to be in 54-59: need to shift right by 5-10 columns
- Dot at col 52 needs to be in 54-59: need to shift right by 2-7 columns
For the bottom 2 dots to overlap with the yellow vertical bar (cols 51-53):
- Dot at col 46 needs to be in 51-53: need to shift right by 5-7 columns
But the dots are at different columns, so they can't all be in the yellow range at the same time. Unless the yellow shape is wider than I think.
Actually, wait. The yellow horizontal bar is at cols 54-59 (6 columns) and the yellow vertical bar is at cols 51-53 (3 columns). The dots are spaced 3 columns apart (46, 49, 52). If I shift the charcoal so that:
- Dot at col 52 is in the yellow horizontal bar (54-59): shift right by 2-7 columns
- Dot at col 46 (vertical) is in the yellow vertical bar (51-53): shift right by 5-7 columns
If I shift right by 5 columns:
- Dot at col 46 → 51 (in yellow vertical bar 51-53) ✓
- Dot at col 49 → 54 (in yellow horizontal bar 54-59) ✓
- Dot at col 52 → 57 (in yellow horizontal bar 54-59) ✓
- Dot at col 46 (vertical) → 51 (in yellow vertical bar 51-53) ✓
So shifting right by 5 columns would make all dots yellow! But each LEFT action shifts by 3 columns, not 5. So I can't achieve exactly 5 columns.
Let me check: currently the charcoal is at cols 45-53. If I press LEFT once more, it shifts to cols 48-56. The dots would be at:
- Row 46: cols 49, 52, 55
- Rows 49, 52: col 49
Yellow ranges:
- Horizontal: cols 54-59
- Vertical: cols 51-53
Checking:
- Dot at col 49: not in 54-59 ✗
- Dot at col 52: not in 54-59 ✗
- Dot at col 55: in 54-59 ✓
- Dot at col 49 (vertical): not in 51-53 ✗
So after 1 more LEFT, only 1 dot would be yellow.
If I press LEFT 2 more times (total 17 LEFTs from the start of this sequence):
- Charcoal at cols 51-59
- Dots at row 46: cols 52, 55, 58
- Dots at rows 49, 52: col 52
Checking:
- Dot at col 52: not in 54-59 ✗
- Dot at col 55: in 54-59 ✓
- Dot at col 58: in 54-59 ✓
- Dot at col 52 (vertical): not in 51-53 ✗ (wait, 52 is in 51-53, so ✓)
So after 2 more LEFTs, 3 dots would be yellow (cols 55, 58 horizontal, col 52 vertical).
Hmm, but the vertical bar of the charcoal would be at cols 51-53, which overlaps with the yellow vertical bar at cols 51-53. So the dots in the vertical bar (col 52) would be yellow.
Let me try pressing LEFT 2 more times and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model:
- After 15 LEFTs: charcoal L-shape at cols 45-53, overlapping with yellow shape.
- Yellow shape split: horizontal bar cols 54-59, vertical bar cols 51-53.
- 1 dot in charcoal turned yellow (col 49). Goal: get all 5 dots yellow.
- Need to shift charcoal right by ~5 columns for optimal overlap.
- Each LEFT shifts by 3 columns. Need 2 more LEFTs to get closer.
Plan: Press LEFT 2 more times, then SPACE to check if level completes.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Press LEFT 2 more times
result = action(['LEFT', 'LEFT'])
print(f"After 2x LEFT: done={result.get('done')}, level_completed={result.get('level_completed')}, board_changed={result.get('board_changed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print(f"\nNodes:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ('B', 'c', 'Y', 'W') and n['pixels'] > 3:
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check for white/charcoal dots
w_dots = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'W']
c_dots = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] <= 2]
y_dots = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'Y' and n['pixels'] <= 2]
print(f"\nW dots: {len(w_dots)}, c dots: {len(c_dots)}, Y dots: {len(y_dots)}")

# Check ASCII around overlap
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nASCII rows 45-53, cols 48-60:")
for r in range(45, 54):
    row_str = ascii_lines[r] if r < len(ascii_lines) else ""
    print(f"  row {r}: {row_str[48:61]}")

# Try SPACE
result = action(['SPACE'])
print(f"\nSPACE: done={result.get('done')}, level_completed={result.get('level_completed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

  B: pixels=64, boundary=[[18, 45], [18, 53], [26, 53], [27, 54], [27, 59], [29, 59], [29, 51], [21, 51], [20, 50], [20, 45]]
  Y: pixels=72, boundary=[[42, 9], [42, 17], [44, 17], [44, 12], [45, 11], [53, 11], [53, 3], [51, 3], [51, 8], [50, 9]]
  B: pixels=63, boundary=[[63, 0], [63, 62]]

W dots: 21, c dots: 0, Y dots: 0

ASCII rows 45-53, cols 48-60:
  row 45: bbbbbbbbbbbbb
  row 46: bbbbbbbbbbbbb
  row 47: bbbbbbbbbbbbb
  row 48: bbbbbbbbbbbbb
  row 49: bbbbbbbbbbbbb
  row 50: bbbbbbbbbbbbb
  row 51: bbbbbbbbbbbbb

SPACE: done=False, level_completed=True

c_dots = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] <= 2]

for r in range(45, 54):
    row_str = ascii_lines[r] if r < len(ascii_lines) else ""
    print(f"  row {r}: {row_str[48:61]}")

# Try SPACE

print(f"\nSPACE: done={result.get('done')}, level_completed={result.get('level_completed')}")
